book_claims.mechanism: Part of an ancestral dietary pattern associated with longevity in Okinawa; exact mechanisms not specified
book_claims.text: Seaweeds are mentioned as a traditional staple of the Okinawan diet, which is associated with extreme longevity

book_claims.mechanism: Extraordinarily concentrated non-heme iron source; provides systemic iron to prevent anemia and support energy metabolism
book_claims.text: Spirulina (seaweed) is the top food source of iron: 31.92 mg per cup dried (177% DV)

supplementary_research.finding: Fucoidan from brown seaweed (PMID 22366739) activates autophagy pathways and shows anti-tumor activity in cell and animal models, supporting longevity-related mechanisms
Spirulina supplementation (PMID 28300263) reduced oxidative stress markers and improved lipid profiles in a randomized trial, consistent with its role as a micronutrient-dense food
Spirulina contains phycocyanin (powerful antioxidant), complete protein (~60% by dry weight), and iodine; evidence supports modest lipid-lowering and antioxidant effects in humans

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Non-heme iron; pair with vitamin C to enhance absorption ~3-fold
Highly bioavailable; excess iodine (especially from kelp) can disrupt thyroid function
Water-soluble antioxidant pigment; inhibits NF-κB inflammatory pathway
Sulfated polysaccharide; partially absorbed; activates immune and autophagy pathways
Plant-form omega-3; conversion to EPA/DHA is limited (~5–15%)

synergies.note: Vitamin C from lemon significantly enhances absorption of non-heme iron in seaweed/spirulina
Fat-soluble carotenoids in seaweed are better absorbed with sesame oil's monounsaturated fats
Traditional pairing in miso soup; together provide complete amino acids, iodine, and calcium
